evidence: Java naming conventions are internally consistent: PascalCase classes, camelCase methods/fields, all lowercase package names with underscore separator (word_sketch).

Handler classes in api package are uniformly package-private (no public modifier) — ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers — one consistent pattern.

Mixed accessor naming in model package: traditional class ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult use getX() prefixed accessors; record types AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge use record-style accessors without prefix. This is documented in package-info.java but creates visible inconsistency.

QueryResults.ConcordanceResult interface requires getSentence(), getStartOffset(), getEndOffset(), getDocId() using getX() convention on an interface; records in QueryResults implement these with @Override — consistent within the sealed type hierarchy.

Two parallel options types: ExplorationOptions and SingleSeedExplorationOptions — both are public records in the exploration package with overlapping field names but different field counts. Their Javadoc field names are identical for shared fields.
ExploreResponseAssembler has both a populateExploreResponse() method and a populateComparisonResponse() method — different naming patterns (populate vs format) but not a drift across sibling classes.

RelationUtils uses a non-final class (not final) despite having only static methods and a private constructor — inconsistent with other utility classes (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, RelationPatternBuilder) which are all final.

strengths: Java naming conventions (PascalCase, camelCase) are consistent throughout all packages.
Handler class visibility (package-private) is applied uniformly across all api handler classes.
package-info.java explicitly documents the mixed record/class accessor convention in the model package.
Utility class pattern (private constructor + static methods) is used consistently in CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, RelationPatternBuilder.
convention_outlier.issue_character: The main drift is in the model package, where traditional classes (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult) and record types mix two accessor naming conventions in the same package, and RelationUtils is the sole non-final utility class among a set of otherwise-final utilities.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is largely convention-consistent — the Java naming and visibility patterns hold across sibling classes. The score penalty comes from the mixed getX()/record-accessor naming in the model package (which creates predictability friction even when documented) and the RelationUtils non-final outlier. Score placed at 73: mostly consistent with identifiable but bounded outliers.

issues.summary: model package mixes getX() accessors (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult) with record-style accessors (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun) in the same package
evidence: AdjectiveProfile.adjective(), nounScores(), presentInCount() — record convention without get prefix.
issues.suggestion: Convert ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult to records (if computed accessors can be expressed as default methods or companion utilities), or document the distinction more prominently. If conversion is not feasible, ensure new model types consistently follow one convention.

issues.summary: RelationUtils is not declared final despite being a static utility class, unlike all sibling utility classes
evidence: RelationUtils: 'public class RelationUtils { private RelationUtils() {} ... }' — no final modifier.
CqlUtils: 'public final class CqlUtils { private CqlUtils() {} ... }' — final.
LogDiceCalculator: 'public class LogDiceCalculator' — also not final, same pattern.
issues.suggestion: Add 'final' to RelationUtils and LogDiceCalculator class declarations to match CqlUtils and RelationPatternBuilder and signal that these utility classes are not designed for extension.

root_causes: Model types were added at different times: earlier types were classes, later additions used the record syntax available in Java 16+. The mixed convention was not retroactively unified.
